# Releasing

Omi v4 ships three independent release streams. Each one has its own tag pattern and its
own workflow; tagging one stream never builds the others.

| Stream | Tag pattern | Workflow | Runs on |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Firmware | `firmware-v*` | [`workflows/release-firmware.yml`](workflows/release-firmware.yml) | `ubuntu-24.04` inside `ghcr.io/nrfconnect/sdk-nrf-toolchain:v2.9.0` |
| Mobile | `mobile-v*` | [`workflows/release-mobile.yml`](workflows/release-mobile.yml) | `ubuntu-24.04` (Android) and `macos-15` (iOS) |
| Desktop | `desktop-v*` | [`workflows/release-desktop.yml`](workflows/release-desktop.yml) | `macos-15` |

`workflows/ci.yml` is unchanged and still runs the quality gates on every push and pull
request. The release workflows are additive and never run on plain pushes.

Every workflow also has a `workflow_dispatch` trigger with an optional `version` input.
A manual run builds and uploads the artifacts as workflow artifacts but does **not**
create a GitHub Release — only a tag push does that.

## Cutting a release

```sh
git tag firmware-v1.4.0 && git push origin firmware-v1.4.0
git tag mobile-v1.4.0   && git push origin mobile-v1.4.0
git tag desktop-v1.4.0  && git push origin desktop-v1.4.0
```

The version label used in artifact filenames is the tag with its stream prefix stripped,
so `firmware-v1.4.0` produces `omi-cv1-1.4.0-dfu_application.zip`.

Release notes are auto-generated by `softprops/action-gh-release@v2`
(`generate_release_notes: true`). Every release also carries a `SHA256SUMS.txt` covering
all of its artifacts.

To dry-run a stream without publishing, use *Actions → Release … → Run workflow* and pass
a `version` such as `dev`. The artifacts land under the run's Artifacts section.

## Secrets

No secret is required to get a build out of any stream. Every signing step checks its
secrets first and, when they are missing, prints a `::notice::` and produces an unsigned
artifact instead of failing. Forks therefore still get usable downloads.

### Firmware — no secrets

The firmware stream needs nothing beyond the default `GITHUB_TOKEN`.

### Mobile

| Secret | Contents | Used for |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_BASE64` | Base64 of the release `.jks`/`.keystore` file (`base64 -i release.jks`) | Re-signing the APK and AAB |
| `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD` | Keystore password | Re-signing the APK and AAB |
| `ANDROID_KEY_ALIAS` | Alias of the signing key inside the keystore | Re-signing the APK and AAB |
| `ANDROID_KEY_PASSWORD` | Key password; falls back to `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD` when unset | Re-signing the APK and AAB |
| `IOS_CERTIFICATE_P12_BASE64` | Base64 of the exported iOS distribution certificate `.p12` | Building a signed `.ipa` |
| `IOS_CERTIFICATE_PASSWORD` | Password protecting that `.p12` | Building a signed `.ipa` |
| `IOS_PROVISIONING_PROFILE_BASE64` | Base64 of the `.mobileprovision` matching the app id and certificate | Building a signed `.ipa` |
| `IOS_EXPORT_METHOD` | `app-store`, `ad-hoc`, `enterprise` or `development`; defaults to `app-store` | Export options plist |
| `APPLE_TEAM_ID` | 10-character Apple Developer team id | Export options plist |

All four Android secrets (alias, keystore, keystore password) must be present or the
Android signing step is skipped. All of `IOS_CERTIFICATE_P12_BASE64`,
`IOS_CERTIFICATE_PASSWORD`, `IOS_PROVISIONING_PROFILE_BASE64` and `APPLE_TEAM_ID` must be
present or the iOS job builds an unsigned `Runner.app` only.

### Desktop

| Secret | Contents | Used for |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `MACOS_CERTIFICATE_P12_BASE64` | Base64 of the exported *Developer ID Application* certificate `.p12` | Signing the app and the DMG |
| `MACOS_CERTIFICATE_PASSWORD` | Password protecting that `.p12` | Signing the app and the DMG |
| `MACOS_SIGNING_IDENTITY` | Full identity string, e.g. `Developer ID Application: Example Inc (LZ3NL5434Q)` | `codesign --sign` |
| `APPLE_ID` | Apple ID used for notarization | `xcrun notarytool submit` |
| `APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD` | App-specific password for that Apple ID | `xcrun notarytool submit` |
| `APPLE_TEAM_ID` | 10-character Apple Developer team id | `xcrun notarytool submit` |

The repository signs with a development certificate locally. In CI, when the three
`MACOS_*` secrets are absent the app is ad-hoc signed (`codesign --sign -`) and the
artifacts are named `…-macos-unsigned.zip` / `…-macos-unsigned.dmg`. Gatekeeper will then
require a right-click → Open on first launch. Notarization additionally needs the three
Apple secrets; without them the notarize and staple steps are skipped.

Never commit a certificate, keystore or password. Add them under
*Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions*.

## Firmware artifacts and which device they flash

One `firmware-v*` tag builds every supported device in a matrix, so a single release
covers all of the hardware. Artifact names are `<device>-<version>-<file>`.

| Artifact prefix | Device | Board | Config |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `omi-cv1-` | Omi CV1 pendant (nRF5340) | `omi/nrf5340/cpuapp`, sysbuild + MCUboot | `omi.conf` |
| `omi-devkit-v1-` | Omi DevKit v1 | `xiao_ble/nrf52840/sense` | `prj_xiao_ble_sense_devkitv1.conf` |
| `omi-devkit-v1-spisd-` | Omi DevKit v1 with SPI SD card | `xiao_ble/nrf52840/sense` | `prj_xiao_ble_sense_devkitv1-spisd.conf` |
| `omi-devkit-v2-adafruit-` | Omi DevKit v2 (Adafruit) | `xiao_ble/nrf52840/sense` | `prj_xiao_ble_sense_devkitv2-adafruit.conf` |
| `omiglass-` | omiGlass (ESP32) | PlatformIO | `platformio.ini` |

The omiGlass job is optional: it looks for a `platformio.ini` under `firmware/` and skips
with a notice when the ESP32 target has not been vendored. The Zephyr jobs are not
optional — if a matrix target's config file cannot be found under `firmware/`, that job
fails with an explicit message listing the `.conf` files it did find, rather than
publishing a release that is quietly missing a device.

Per device you get, depending on what the build produces:

- `…-dfu_application.zip` — MCUboot OTA package, for the CV1 pendant.
- `…-merged.hex` — full flash image for a wired J-Link programmer.
- `…-zephyr.hex`, `…-zephyr.bin` — application image only.
- `…-zephyr.uf2` — drag-and-drop image for the XIAO bootloader (DevKit targets).
- `…-app_update.bin` — MCUboot update image, when sysbuild emits one.

### Flashing over the air (recommended for the CV1 pendant)

1. Download `omi-cv1-<version>-dfu_application.zip` from the release. Do not unzip it.
2. Install **nRF Connect for Mobile** (Nordic Semiconductor) on iOS or Android.
3. Put the pendant in range and open nRF Connect → **Scan**, then connect to the device.
4. Open the **DFU** tab, choose **Select File**, pick the `.zip`, and start the update.
5. Leave the phone next to the pendant until the transfer reaches 100% and the device
   reboots on its own.

If the pendant does not advertise, charge it first — a low battery aborts DFU partway.

### Flashing with a J-Link / debug probe

```sh
nrfjprog --program omi-cv1-<version>-merged.hex --chiperase --verify --reset
```

Or with `west` if you have an nRF Connect SDK workspace:

```sh
west flash --hex-file omi-cv1-<version>-merged.hex
```

The merged hex includes MCUboot plus the application, so `--chiperase` is safe and gives
a known-good starting state. Flashing only `…-zephyr.hex` leaves the existing bootloader
in place.

### Flashing a DevKit (XIAO nRF52840 Sense)

Double-tap the reset button to enter the UF2 bootloader, then copy
`omi-devkit-…-zephyr.uf2` onto the mass-storage volume that appears. The board reboots
into the new firmware once the copy completes.

## Caching

- Flutter SDK and pub cache — `subosito/flutter-action@v2` with `cache: true`, plus
  `~/.pub-cache` in the `actions/cache@v4` entries.
- Gradle — `~/.gradle/caches` and `~/.gradle/wrapper`, keyed on the Gradle files.
- Cargo — `~/.cargo/registry`, `~/.cargo/git` and `app/native/hub/target`, keyed on
  `Cargo.toml`.
- CocoaPods — `~/Library/Caches/CocoaPods`, keyed on `Podfile.lock`.
- nRF Connect SDK — the whole `/opt/ncs` west workspace, keyed on the NCS version. The
  first firmware run has to `west init` and `west update` from scratch and will take
  considerably longer than subsequent runs.

## Known rough edges

These workflows have not been executed yet. Expect the first real run of each stream to
need adjustment:

- The NCS container's toolchain may not be on `PATH` for non-entrypoint steps. The
  firmware workflow has a fallback that discovers `/opt/nordic/ncs/toolchains/*` and
  appends it to `$GITHUB_PATH`; if the image layout differs the job fails with an
  explicit message.
- The devkit matrix entries assume `firmware/devkit/` and in-tree
  `xiao_ble/nrf52840/sense`. The layout check falls back to searching for the config file
  anywhere under `firmware/`, but the board name may still need correcting against
  `firmware/README.md`.
- `flutter build ipa` uses `signingStyle: manual` while the Xcode project is set to
  automatic signing; the first signed iOS run may need the export options or the project
  configuration reconciled.
- Android release signing is done by re-signing the built APK/AAB with `apksigner` and
  `jarsigner`, because `app/android/app/build.gradle.kts` still points its release build
  type at the debug signing config. Wiring a real `signingConfig` into Gradle is the
  cleaner long-term fix.
- macOS `flutter build macos --release` runs with the project's automatic signing and a
  `DEVELOPMENT_TEAM` set; if the build itself fails on a runner without a certificate,
  the fix is to make the Xcode project fall back to ad-hoc signing rather than to change
  this workflow.
